data.title: Front-end Developer at NQode
data.subtitle: April 2024 – May 2026
data.excerpt: Built scalable front-end architectures using Angular and Next.js, with a focus on modular design, performance, and maintainability. Led initiatives to streamline development through reusable components, coding standards, and CI/CD workflows, resulting in faster feature delivery and improved team efficiency.

context.lede: nQode is a software development company specialising in custom digital products built through dedicated and managed engineering teams. Based in Novi Sad, they work with startups, scaleups, and enterprises across the full project lifecycle — from early ideation through to large-scale deployment.
body: Their core offering spans web and mobile development, enterprise applications, SaaS products, and MVP builds — with flexible engagement models ranging from single-developer extensions to full multi-team arrangements.

Client relationships are built around reliability and long-term partnership. nQode positions itself as a trusted delivery partner across all stages of a product, not just a vendor for isolated engagements.

scope.title: A scalable front-end built to outlive the next rewrite.
scope.body: Designed and implemented a modular Angular + TypeScript architecture with a strong focus on long-term maintainability and a clear path for future feature expansion.

scope.title: A reusable component library that the product moves faster on.
scope.body: Built and maintained a shared UI library aligned with design-system principles — guaranteeing consistent behaviour across products and meaningfully accelerating feature delivery.

scope.title: Figma to pixel-perfect, accessible interface — without losing the brief.
scope.body: Translated complex product requirements into responsive, accessible UI that holds up against the source design and against real users on real devices.

scope.title: Front-ends that talk to messy back-ends, cleanly.
scope.body: Integrated apps with REST and GraphQL APIs, designing data flows that stay responsive under load and keep state predictable across long user sessions.

scope.title: Linting, standards, peer review.
scope.body: Introduced linting rules, coding standards, and structured peer review — measurably improving code quality and shortening onboarding for new engineers.

scope.title: Tight loops with design, back-end and product.
scope.body: Working closely with designers, back-end engineers and product to rapidly prototype, iterate, and ship high-quality features in a fast-paced environment.

pullQuote.text: Build the boring infrastructure first. Everything ships faster after that.

work.title: Source AG
work.blurb: Embedded across multiple front-end teams at a large-scale greenhouse intelligence platform — contributed to data-dense dashboards and cultivation tooling used by commercial growers across Europe.

work.title: BrightFunded
work.blurb: Sole front-end owner for a funded trading platform — built and owned the entire client-facing UI from the ground up, across trader evaluation flows, dashboards, and account management.

work.title: Owow
work.blurb: Built high-fidelity, animation-driven websites for a digital agency known for pushing the visual bar — precise GSAP work, CMS-driven content, and a strong focus on motion quality.

approach.title: Maintainability is a feature.
approach.body: If the next engineer can't ship on top of it cheaply, it doesn't matter how nice it looked on the demo. I write for the team that inherits the code, not the team that wrote it.

approach.title: Design and engineering are one loop.
approach.body: The cleanest interfaces I've shipped came from short, opinionated loops with design — not from waterfall hand-offs. I push back on patterns that won't scale, and ship the ones that do.

approach.title: Boring tools, ambitious work.
approach.body: I pick mature, predictable tooling so the interesting effort goes into the product. Angular, TypeScript, sensible CSS — the surprises should come from the design, not the build pipeline.

data.title: Frontend Developer at EPAM Systems
data.subtitle: February 2022 – April 2024
data.excerpt: Implemented dynamic, responsive front-end features with Angular and Sass, enhancing UX through polished UI components and animations. Optimized deployment with Docker and fostered strong cross-functional collaboration to ensure timely, aligned project delivery.

context.lede: EPAM Systems is a global software engineering and IT services company with operations across 50+ countries and tens of thousands of engineers. They partner with Fortune 500 companies, technology leaders, and fast-growing businesses to deliver large-scale software products, digital transformation, and platform engineering.
body: Their core offering spans custom software development, cloud and infrastructure services, data and AI, digital experience design, and quality engineering — often operating as the dedicated engineering arm for clients who need serious delivery capacity.

EPAM is known for structured, process-driven engineering at scale — distributed teams, rigorous QA practices, and long-running client engagements that demand consistency and reliability across time zones and disciplines.

scope.title: Dynamic, responsive features shipped consistently and on schedule.
scope.body: Built and iterated on front-end features across multiple client products, balancing responsiveness, cross-browser compatibility, and adherence to design specifications.

scope.title: Polished components that elevated the product's perceived quality.
scope.body: Developed reusable Angular components and refined micro-animations that brought designs to life and measurably improved the user experience.

scope.title: Scalable SCSS architectures built for long-running projects.
scope.body: Maintained and extended modular Sass structures — ensuring visual consistency across large codebases without the usual accumulation of overrides and dead styles.

scope.title: Deployment optimised with Docker-based workflows.
scope.body: Contributed to Docker-based development and deployment pipelines, reducing environment inconsistencies and improving reliability from local to production.

scope.title: Cross-functional coordination across time zones.
scope.body: Worked closely with designers, back-end engineers, QA and product owners across distributed teams — maintaining clear communication and ensuring timely, well-aligned delivery.

pullQuote.text: Ship features. Keep the codebase in a state you'd be happy to inherit.

work.title: Meridianbet
work.lede: A high-traffic sports betting platform serving the Western Balkans market — a live, stateful product with real-time odds, complex betting flows, and zero tolerance for UI regressions.
work.blurb: Delivered front-end features across a large Angular codebase for a live sports betting platform, working within a structured cross-functional team under tight QA and delivery processes.

breakdown.label: Challenge
breakdown.text: A feature-dense, high-traffic betting interface with live data requirements and an established codebase that needed continuous development without disrupting active users.

breakdown.label: Approach
breakdown.text: Developed and iterated on UI features within a disciplined Angular architecture, working in close daily loops with 4 QAs, backend engineers, and a PM — maintaining code quality through structured reviews.

breakdown.label: Outcome
breakdown.text: Consistent on-schedule feature delivery across a two-year engagement on a live, high-visibility product serving a large regional market.

approach.title: Consistency compounds.
approach.body: In large enterprise codebases, the biggest wins come from getting small things right, every time — naming, structure, review. Inconsistency is the real technical debt.

approach.title: Distributed teams need over-communication.
approach.body: Across time zones and client boundaries, explicit alignment beats assumed alignment. I keep documentation tight and communication proactive.

approach.title: Quality has a schedule.
approach.body: Shipping on time and shipping well aren't mutually exclusive — but it takes discipline upfront. I scope carefully and flag risks early, not at the last sprint.

data.title: Frontend Developer at Codeplicity.
data.subtitle: February 2021 – February 2022
data.excerpt: Developed interactive web features using vanilla JavaScript, PHP, and HTML with a focus on performance and user experience. Employed modular SASS styling and Vagrant-based environments to ensure maintainability, visual consistency, and smooth team collaboration.

context.lede: Codeplicity is a web development agency building bespoke digital products for clients across various industries. My first professional role, where I built a solid foundation in front-end fundamentals — semantic HTML, performant JavaScript, and scalable CSS.
body: The work was broad and hands-on: feature development, bug fixing, and performance improvements across a range of client websites and web applications built on PHP backends.
Working in a small, close-knit team meant wearing multiple hats — from implementing designs pixel-by-pixel to managing local development environments and contributing to shared code standards.

scope.title: Interactive web features built with clean, performant JavaScript.
scope.body: Implemented dynamic UI features using vanilla JavaScript — carousels, modals, form validation, animations — with a focus on performance and cross-browser compatibility.

scope.title: Modular SASS that keeps visual consistency across a growing codebase.
scope.body: Structured stylesheets using modular SASS patterns — variables, mixins, and component-level files — ensuring visual consistency and making future maintenance manageable.

scope.title: PHP templates turned into polished, accessible interfaces.
scope.body: Worked within PHP-driven template systems to deliver clean, semantically correct HTML output — keeping markup meaningful and accessibility-aware from the start.

scope.title: Vagrant setups that made local development reliable for the whole team.
scope.body: Set up and maintained Vagrant-based development environments, keeping local stacks consistent with production and reducing the classic 'works on my machine' friction.

scope.title: Small team, high ownership, quick feedback loops.
scope.body: Collaborated daily with designers and back-end developers in a small studio setting — fast iteration, direct feedback, and a strong sense of shared ownership over every deliverable.

pullQuote.text: Learn the fundamentals deeply enough and everything else becomes a variation.

approach.title: Start with the fundamentals.
approach.body: Before reaching for a framework or library, I try to understand what the browser already gives you. Most UI problems have native solutions that are simpler, faster and more accessible.

approach.title: Make it work, then make it right.
approach.body: Get a working version in front of real eyes quickly, then use that feedback to refine. A rough prototype teaches you more than a week of design reviews.

approach.title: Own the full surface.
approach.body: In a small team, specialisation is a luxury. Learning to move across the stack — HTML, CSS, JS, templating, environment setup — gave me a broader picture of how things fit together.
